era_context: The early 14th century continued to be a period of transition and crisis across Eurasia. The Mongol Empire's fragmentation was accelerating, with the Yuan Dynasty in China facing mounting internal pressures. Ottoman expansion in Anatolia was proceeding rapidly. In Europe, the Hundred Years' War was approaching (1337), and feudal instability persisted. The Islamic world remained culturally and intellectually vibrant despite political fragmentation.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern and Eastern Europe beyond major kingdoms

events.title: Death of Osman I — Succession of Orhan I in Ottoman Empire
events.description: Osman I, the founder of the Ottoman Empire, died in 1323 or 1324 (sources vary). By 1330, his son Orhan I had consolidated power and was actively expanding Ottoman territory. Orhan I (r. 1323–1362) transformed the Ottoman principality from a small frontier state into a major regional power. In 1330, Orhan was in the midst of his military campaigns to conquer Byzantine territories in Bithynia and Thrace. His reign marked the transition from the early Ottoman state under Osman to a more institutionalised empire with a standing army, administrative structure, and legal code. Orhan's consolidation of power and territorial expansion laid the foundation for Ottoman dominance in the eastern Mediterranean and Balkans.
events.certainty_note: Osman I's death date is traditionally given as 1323 or 1324, but some sources place it as late as 1326. Orhan's succession and early reign are well-attested, but precise dating of events in the 1320s–1330s is uncertain by ±2–3 years.

events.title: Capture of Bursa — Ottoman conquest of major Byzantine city
events.description: Ottoman forces under Orhan I captured the Byzantine city of Bursa (ancient Prusa) in 1326 or 1330 (sources differ). Bursa was one of the most important Byzantine cities in Anatolia and had served as a temporary capital of the Byzantine Empire in the 14th century. The capture of Bursa was a major strategic victory for the Ottomans, giving them control of a wealthy, well-fortified urban centre and a base for further expansion. Bursa became the first Ottoman capital and remained a major centre of Ottoman power and culture. The conquest demonstrated Ottoman military capability and marked a significant step in the decline of Byzantine territorial control in Anatolia.
events.certainty_note: The capture of Bursa is traditionally dated to 1326, but some sources place it in 1330 or even 1331. The uncertainty reflects the difficulty of dating Ottoman military campaigns from later chronicles. The event itself is confirmed, but the precise year is disputed by ±4 years.
cross_references: Part of Ottoman expansion in Anatolia (1320s–1360s)

events.title: Reign of Philip VI of France — Early Hundred Years' War tensions
events.description: Philip VI of Valois (r. 1328–1350) was king of France in 1330, having recently ascended to the throne in 1328 following the death of Charles IV. In 1330, Philip VI was consolidating his authority and beginning to assert French claims over Aquitaine and other English-held territories. His reign would lead directly to the outbreak of the Hundred Years' War in 1337, when Edward III of England challenged Philip's right to the French throne. In 1330, tensions between France and England were rising but had not yet erupted into open warfare. Philip's reign was marked by attempts to strengthen royal authority and expand French territorial control.
key_figures: Philip VI of Valois (King of France, r. 1328–1350)

sources.name: French royal records and chronicles (Froissart, Grandes Chroniques de France)

events.certainty_note: Philip VI's reign and the political situation in 1330 are well-documented in contemporary and near-contemporary sources.

events.title: Continued Yuan Dynasty instability — Famine and rebellion
events.description: The Yuan Dynasty continued to experience severe famines, epidemics, and social unrest in 1330. The combination of natural disasters, administrative corruption, and Mongol factional conflicts created widespread suffering and fuelled peasant rebellions. These conditions were documented in Yuan official records and later Chinese histories. The famines and epidemics of 1330–1331 are among the earliest documented outbreaks of plague in China and may represent the initial stages of the Black Death pandemic. The social and demographic crisis of this period contributed significantly to the eventual collapse of Yuan rule and the rise of the Ming Dynasty.
key_figures: Toghon Temür (Emperor Wenzong of Yuan, r. 1328–1332)

sources.name: Yuan Shi (Official History of the Yuan Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: Famines and epidemics in Yuan China in 1330 are well-documented in multiple sources.
cross_references: Part of Black Death pandemic origins

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Bursa's capture is disputed. While traditionally assigned to 1326, some modern scholars argue for 1330 or even later dates based on reanalysis of Ottoman and Byzantine sources. The discrepancy reflects the unreliability of Ottoman chronicles for precise dating of early Ottoman military campaigns. Additionally, the strategic significance of Bursa's capture has been somewhat overstated in traditional narratives; while important, it was one of several Ottoman conquests and did not immediately lead to Ottoman dominance of the region.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1330 is similar to 1331: European sources are relatively abundant for political and military history, Chinese sources are well-preserved but often compiled later, and Islamic/Ottoman sources are later compilations. The record is heavily weighted toward elite history and military campaigns. Economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. Sub-Saharan Africa, Oceania, and Central Asia remain essentially undocumented.

graph_edges.note: Orhan I's consolidation of power and military expansion occurred simultaneously
Bursa's capture was part of the long Ottoman expansion that eventually led to the conquest of Constantinople
Philip VI's consolidation of power and territorial assertions led directly to the outbreak of the Hundred Years' War
Yuan famines and epidemics of 1330 are part of the early stages of the Black Death pandemic
